sentences.text: Still not my, eh, war experiences?
What-- what you are asking now?
Still, during the war, not--
Yes.
It means, uh, if I do remember exactly, eh, the war started in '39.
September.
That's when my-- my daughter was born.
In-- But.
She was born in March, '39.
Yes.
I think I did.
That a lot of-- eh, most people were Zionists.
It was a small town, not far from Lwow.
Lwow was the largest city in that area.
And, eh, most people-- not only young people, you know, adults-- belonged to the Tarbut and, eh, quite a number of, eh, organizations for younger people-- for younger and young people.
I was then in Hashomer Hatzair, since my early teens.
Already, I was 10, 11, 12-- started Hashomer Hatzair.
I-- actually, I was brought up in Hashomer Hatzair.
And I still feel it here.
And that was more, eh, Labor Zionists-- leftists.
Eh, yes, but, uh, there was no time to work as a nurse.
I-- I think that the-- in the fall, the questions I put in were to do how-- where-- how-- where did you work, and what did you do before the war?
And I put down nothing, but I didn't mean that I worked.
Because when the Russians came, eh, they needed some people, not, eh, nurses.
They had their Russians.
They brought over their Russian nurses.
But I worked in a laboratory there.
And I remember exactly where it was.
The polytechnic, eh, was converted into a laboratory.
And that was on-- in Lwow on [NON-ENGLISH] Street, [NON-ENGLISH], where I worked.
And I worked only part-time.
Because had a small child.
And I had to-- also, I didn't want to, but I had to give it to a-- eh, it's not like here, a kindergarten.
That was something like a kindergarten.
And they kept her for a few hours afternoon, until, from my work, I came, picked her up there and took her home.
But we had to work, because, eh, the Russians took away our homes-- homes, houses, or flats, wherever one lived.
And they took-- because they came with their families, with their wives, And they couldn't get anything empty houses there.
So they took over, took them away from us, left us in the one little room only in the kitchen.
And they took-- if one had a three-roomed or four-roomed, eh, flat, or bigger house, they took away everything, and they lived the time there, because it was a lot of them.
They came, eh, not only near, the Russians, from Lwow, like, over the border, but from distant places in Russia, even from, eh, mm, Kurdistan, Dagestan, and all those, eh, places in Russia.
So we had some-- [NON-ENGLISH] us over.
And they took away from us everything.
So they left me-- but if one worked, then they left a room over for her, because we worked for them.
That's why it was-- I had to work a half a day, part-time.
Otherwise, it were very hard at this time-- very hard.
We had to stay-- to get up very early.
And there were queues for food.
Not much food there, because they needed everything for the-- for their families, the Russians needed.
Eh, so we had to get up early in the morning, with a child in my arms, before going to work, and to stand in a queue.

Queues-- there were queues up to, eh, 50, 60, or 80 people waiting, one by one, to get into the store there, whatever it was, eh, a shop, to get a little bit of sugar-- about-- not even a quarter, or-- very little, they gave us.
And bread-- that was the most what we got-- bread and sugar.
But because I worked, at work, we got it.
We got a little bit more-- people working.
So everybody wanted to get to work, because they gave us, eh, a bit of food for the child, or for myself.
Not much was available.
If you wanted to-- to buy a pair of shoes, you had to work a whole month to get a pair of shoes.
Otherwise, you couldn't get it.
That's only, eh, eh, to-- to say-- otherwise, we couldn't get.
There was no clothing.
You couldn't get anything.
And whatever we had in the shops from before the war, they took out everything.
They took it away from us.
So one couldn't get much there.
What?
I don't know.
What do you mean it was, what?
Typical?
You mean the people were undernourished?
Because they didn't have--
They didn't have much, no.
I was very thin there.
But it was still better than at the-- when the Germans came, because, with the Germans, we had nothing at all.
It was very hard with the-- with the Soviets, but still better than with the Germans.
At least, they didn't kill us.
No, not much.
No.
We couldn't-- we couldn't-- we couldn't buy it.
It was very hard to buy, unless it was on the-- illegally bought.
You call it, eh, black-- on the black market.
That's how they call it.
We used to go to the market on-- on a Saturday when I didn't work.
And-- but you had to have money for it.
On the black market, everything, eh, costs about three or four times more.
And not many people had-- was left with money there.
If they-- there's-- there were some people who had money before the war, but it was in the banks.
But the Russians took over everything, so they didn't get it.
I don't know.
Maybe some people remember.
I don't remember much.
I know, rather, people in the country could get more.
But the Russians, as I remember, eh, took away from the peasants everything too.
They took their fields over-- they took over everything.
So with whatever they had, any quantities of food left, they took it away from them.
For instance, my, eh, husband-- my late husband's grandfather had, in Grodek, not far from Lwow, a mill.
I think I mentioned before something-- flour mill.
So they took away-- they-- they took over the mill.
He had nothing left.
So we couldn't get flour.
We couldn't get anything.
It was a very-- life was very hard, but we didn't know what is waiting for us with the Germans.
At least, they didn't kill us.
And they respected old people.
They were very helpful.
If they have seen on the street an old person, they went over and helped them to get over the road, if cars were there or--
--trucks-- the Russians.
The Russians were very, very helpful.
I mean, we didn't have enough food, because maybe they didn't have it either.
It was a war, and everything was confiscated.
But they were very kind people, very kind to-- especially, to old people and to children.
And I remember that, when they came over to my place-- I was on the second floor, as I said, on [INAUDIBLE], in Lwow.
And their officers knocked on-- as they knocked, we had to come straightaway.
I opened the door.
And my husband was not at home.
But soon, I lost him, because the Russians took him away.
They needed him much earlier then they took people into the war, men, especially, much, much early, because, as I mentioned, he an engineer.
And they needed him for machines, tanks, building machines, or something like it.
And I remember-- my daughter never remembers her father-- when they came over and took him.
And he didn't know where they would take him.
And I didn't.
He couldn't tell me anything, He just said, I will write-- I will write-- and left me penniless.
They-- the Russians, where he works with them for a short time before they took him, they said to my husband, they will look after me, and they will pay me monthly, or weekly, whatever.
They never did.
They promised, but they never did.
They didn't get-- I didn't get-- for his work, for months that they owed him, they didn't pay him everything at once.
But I don't know.
I don't remember exactly, monthly or weekly.
They owed him quite a lot, and they didn't pay it.
And they took him away.
For a long time, I didn't hear from him anything.
And about a month later, or over a month later, I got the first letter.
I recognized-- recognized his handwriting, but there was no address.
There was a number, only.
I had no idea what it is, because I found out-- I asked Russians who lived at me-- with me in my flat, who took over, I asked them what it means, where's it from?
They said, I'm not allowed to tell you.
And he was not allowed to write you-- to you, because where he works, one shouldn't know where it is.
So later, it stopped.
I didn't get any letters.
And that was until the Germans came.
When did the Russians take him?
Was that the end of '39, or later?
The Russians came in '39.
They took him about two months later.
That was three, two and a half months later.
Where they took him, I don't know.
I don't remember, exactly.
When-- it was over two months later, since they arrived, they came.
I never thought I'd have to remember that.
My brother lived with a young wife, with a two-months, or three-months-old child, not far from me in Lwow, not far from me-- my eldest brother.
Because, as I mentioned, I had later a little brother, the youngest.
And my sister, who came from Israel, as I mentioned before, too, she lived with her husband and her child not far-- nearer even to me-- over the street.
I had to walk over on the other side.
And she lived there.
She was trapped, as I said, in Lwow, with her husband and a child.
The child was born in Lwow.
And the other, eh, part of the family, my parents-- yes, and a-- a younger sister, who survived the war.
She lives in France.
She lived with my parents in, eh, Grodek, not far from Lwow.
But she stayed with us from time to time.
She came over, and she stayed with us in Lwow.
So actually, in Grodek, were only my parents, one other sister, older sister, who was born in '14, and the youngest.
But that sister got ill.
I don't remember what it was, because she was in Grodek.
And my parents brought her later on to a hospital to Lwow.
And, eh, we never heard of her.
When the Germans came, they killed all the inmates of the hospital.
And she was killed.
So there were left only my parents and my youngest brother.
But I didn't tell, exactly, the story where they stay-- were hidden during the war.
A priest took them in and hid them in a cellar there.
Because, eh, where I was born, where I lived all-- all the years, school years, we lived not far from, eh, church.
And, eh, it was actually an orthodox church, Ukrainian.
And that priest, my mother knew him very well.
They were neighbors.
We lived there.
As I mentioned before, eh, we lived on a street-- there were not many Jews in Grodek.
And there was a church not far.
And he took in my parents and hid them in a cellar.
So it was only my father, mother, and the youngest brother.
They were-- I don't know exactly, because I wasn't there, but my mother told me.
She managed to come to come to Lwow later on, before the ghetto in Grodek was-- where they killed them all, finished off.
So, eh, I think there were a few-- I don't know exactly-- about two months hidden there.

And they would probably survive there, but they didn't, because, eh, a boy who worked for that priest, um, in the sheds with cows, or something-- I don't know how they called them-- he noticed once that the priest-- or his wife, I don't know who-- took some food [INAUDIBLE] down to the cellar.
He understood that there were hidden Jews, so he went over to the Gestapo and told them.
The Gestapo came and took them out, all of them.
But the ghetto was still there, so they took them to the ghetto.
And my little brother-- Grodek was a small town, a very pretty town.
There was, eh, the center of the little city, a square center in the middle of the city.
And they called out all non-Jews around, put them around.
They stood around, hundreds of them.
And in the center, they put my little brother.
Yes, and my mother had to stay-- including my mother, had to be there too.
She had to-- they made her watch what they did.
And they hit my brother, that little boy of 13-- he was a bar mitzvah then.
He should have been.
They hit him.
They beat him so hard, until he was a mass-- mess of blood.
He fell.
And my mother said he still screamed mama.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And she couldn't avert her eyes, because they made her look.
If she looked away, they turned her head to see what they are doing.
I don't know what happened.
I think his bones must be somewhere there.
That was, I think, in '41, or end of '41, because the Russians were '39, '40, till '41.
They begin-- it must be-- it must have been middle-- if I do remember exactly, I don't know exactly-- it must have been '41.
The name?
I don't-- I didn't-- I never knew him even.
I have never-- my mother knew him, but I didn't know him.
I don't know if they-- what they did to him, because the people who hid Jews, they killed them too.
I asked my mother.
She said she doesn't know, she-- because he took her away from there.
And there was a part of Grodek where, even before the war, it was like a ghetto.
It was not a ghetto, but like.
There lived only Jews there, in Grodek.
That's where they took my mother and father, before they killed them.
And how they killed them, my parents, [SIGHS] that, I found out later.
I-- I don't know exactly, but I found it out later from a person who survived there.
But not in-- no one survived there.
But he was hidden outside, he and his brother.
They both were hidden outside by peasants.
And he survived, his brother did not.
His brother went out too early after the war and was killed by the [NON-ENGLISH].
You know who these [NON-ENGLISH] were-- Poles.
And his brother survived.
Otherwise, I wouldn't know how my parents died, but-- or were murdered.
But I have been in Israel-- I don't know if I mentioned to you-- a few years, since '76 to '84.
And there, I met that person, who survived, whose brother was killed.
And he was from Grodek.
And he told me how these people-- including my parents, of course-- how they were murdered.
They took them all together, from that ghetto.
I don't know how many could be there-- about a few hundred.
I don't know exactly.
And they marched them-- they had to walk to their deaths.
They had to walk through the city, right-- there was a road.
Eh, that road was [?
Olitza ?] Lwowska.
That's where the gymnasium was.
I told you where I attended it in Lwowska.
And a long road-- and that road led to Lwow.
That's why the name, Lwowska, [?
Olitza ?] Lwowska.
And there was a long way they marched them, they had to walk.
I don't think that my parents or other people-- there was still young people there, because my mother was only in her 50s, early 50s.
And they marched them to a place before Lwow.
I don't know how many kilometers it was.
And I don't think that many people reached that place where they killed them all.
It was a-- [NON-ENGLISH] How do you say a [NON-ENGLISH]?
Forest.
Forest?
A forest?
It-- it wasn't exactly a forest, something like it.
Not a garden, but that was a place between garden and forest-- something like it.
And there, they shot them all, killed them all, one by one.
But how many people came there alive, I don't know.
I think they-- they were undernourished-- not food in the last year.
Even my mother said, when she-- how I knew about my brother-- that was-- before they killed them, they let them stay in the ghetto.
So my mother, somewhere-- I don't know where she got some money, but-- I don't know if my mother was one of them, or-- there were probably more mothers like my mother.
She still, knowing that they will die soon, that they will be killed, she still came to Lwow disguised as a peasant woman, to see what it-- what we are-- how we-- if we are still alive and feel.
And she came in and reached us.
Uh, she should-- I don't know where she took money.

She paid, um, a peasant for, uh, a horse and a, um-- because they didn't have cars at the time in Grodek, and they couldn't go by train, because they had to take a carriage-- not-- it wasn't a carriage, it's a-- it's a--
Something like it, and the horses.
And she came to us, only for half an hour, and told us a story how our little brother-- brother died.
That was in '41, when--
Eh, just before the Germans came in.
The Germans were-- I think the Germans were not far, but she had the courage to do it.
Yes, before the Germans came in.
Eh, he was in Israel, but he-- he's not alive anymore.
He died.
I think it's a natural death, so hard to take something like it.
He's not alive anymore.
He died.
But I knew him very well, because-- eh, Mandel--
--was his name.
No, I don't-- that's his surname, Mandel.
Yes, Mandel.
This, I remember, because his sister was-- two sisters were at school with me.
And he was the eldest-- the eldest brother.
That's why-- and-- and I've seen him.
He heard in [INAUDIBLE] that I survived, that I'm there.
So he came.
He knew that I would like to know about my parents.
So he knew them very well.
Pardon?
Where I lived in Israel?
That-- I-- I lived there too.
It was Tel Aviv.
And he lived in Tel Aviv too.
But he died there, later.
No.
I know the church didn't-- I don't think it had a name.
It was an orthodox-- Ukrainian orthodox church.
Eh, I think, Grodekska, of Grodek.
One was Lawowska, because not far from Lwow.
And I think that was-- I think, Grodekska.
Yes.
Yes.
